title: Decorating polymer clay with alcohol inks
subtitle: Applying and blending alcohol inks on raw or baked polymer clay
excerpt: Alcohol inks flow and blend on polymer clay surfaces in unpredictable, liquid ways that brushwork cannot replicate. Applied to raw clay before baking or to baked clay as a surface finish, they produce the translucent, layered colour of gemstones and marbled stone.
sourceNotes: Alcohol ink on polymer clay is a contemporary craft technique. No historical source; derived from established craft practice.

content.text: Alcohol inks are dye-based inks in an alcohol carrier that dry fast and re-activate with isopropyl alcohol. On

, they move like watercolour but dry to a vibrant permanent colour. The technique is well-suited to earrings, pendants, and sculptural pieces where a natural, variegated surface is wanted. No specialist equipment beyond the inks and polymer clay is needed.

attrs.body: Alcohol inks contain isopropyl alcohol. Work in a ventilated room or with an open window. Do not apply near a flame.
attrs.title: Ventilation note

content.text: Method 1: on raw clay before baking

Condition and shape the polymer clay piece. Drop ink directly from the bottle nozzle onto the clay surface in small drops. Tilt the piece to let the ink run and blend, or use a lint-free cloth to blend two adjacent colours where they meet. Let the ink dry for two to three minutes before adding a second colour. Bake as normal once the ink is fully dry;
seals the ink into the surface.
Method 2: on baked clay as a surface finish

Drop ink onto the cooled baked surface. The ink sits on the surface rather than soaking in, which gives a more controlled, painterly result. For a layered gemstone look: apply a base colour, let dry, add a second colour in a different area, dry, then blend the boundaries with a cloth dampened with a tiny drop of isopropyl alcohol.

Alcohol inks on baked polymer clay are not fully lightfast and can rub off over time. Seal with a polymer clay glaze or acrylic varnish after the ink is fully dry. Matte varnish preserves the natural look; gloss varnish deepens the colour.
What it looks like when it is right

Well-applied alcohol ink looks like a translucent wash of jewel colour with soft-edged pools and organic transitions between hues. It does not look flat or opaque. Over-worked areas turn muddy; the skill is knowing when to stop.
Applying too many colours at once. Three colours maximum; four or more on a small piece usually blends to brown. Plan the palette before opening any ink.
Blending while the ink is still wet. The colours lift and smear rather than blending softly. Let each colour dry before touching it.

items.fix: Wait for each colour to dry before adding the next; limit to two or three hues
Clean the raw clay surface with a lint-free cloth; add a tiny drop of isopropyl alcohol to the ink drops to help them flow
items.cause: Clay surface is too dry or has fingerprint oil on it
items.symptom: Ink does not spread naturally on raw clay
